title: ProtocolLab Runner v1 Architecture
purpose: Describe how Runner v1 satisfies the implementation-neutral orchestration requirements in `SPEC-PL-RUNNER`.
design_summary: RunnerEngine is the orchestration boundary used by the CLI. It receives parsed command intent, asks ManifestCatalog for validated manifests, uses RunPlanBuilder and CompatibilityClassifier to produce executable and non-executable cells, coordinates target lifecycle and readiness, runs validation before any benchmark load, invokes load tools through manifest-defined execution, preserves artifacts through ArtifactCoordinator, classifies evidence and comparability, writes machine-readable plus human-readable report artifacts through ReportCoordinator, and returns structured RunnerCommandResult records plus optional RunnerEvent messages for hosts to render.
key_components: RunnerEngine: owns the runner workflow and exposes orchestration entry points for list, check, validate, run, and report flows that return RunnerCommandResult.
RunnerCommandResult: carries command kind, exit code, structured messages, and artifact references without depending on a CLI console.
RunnerEvent and IRunnerEventSink: provide optional structured status events for hosts that need progress reporting during long-running operations.
ManifestCatalog: loads implementation, scenario, suite, load tool, and network profile manifests into a validated catalog without protocol implementation references.
RunPlanBuilder: resolves suites and command options into deterministic run cells with stable ordering and artifact path inputs.
CompatibilityClassifier: labels cells as runnable, unsupported, unavailable, or incompatible before any target startup.
TargetLifecycleCoordinator: starts, observes, stops, and cleans process, Docker, or external targets through execution backend contracts.
ReadinessCoordinator: waits for manifest-defined readiness probes and turns startup timeout or probe failure into diagnostics.
ValidationCoordinator: runs scenario validators and protocol proof adapters before benchmark data can be accepted.
LoadExecutionCoordinator: invokes process, Docker, or managed load tools from load-tool manifests after validation succeeds.
ArtifactCoordinator: writes manifest snapshots, scenario snapshots, validation output, target diagnostics, raw load-tool output, result JSON, and report artifacts under deterministic paths.
ReportCoordinator: builds aggregate results, evidence classes, comparability status, markdown summaries, and report/index views.
data_and_state_considerations: The run plan, compatibility result, target execution record, readiness result, validation result, load execution result, artifact map, benchmark result, aggregate result, and report summary are separate state records. Each cell result records both accepted measurements and non-accepted outcomes so unsupported or failed cells are visible in aggregate output.
edge_cases_and_constraints: Compatibility filtering happens before target startup so raw QUIC and future-family cells remain explicit non-execution outcomes.
Readiness failure still produces a cell result and preserves target stdout, target stderr, startup metadata, and cleanup status when available.
Validation failure prevents benchmark acceptance even if a load tool can technically run.
Metric parser failure keeps raw stdout and stderr as the authoritative benchmark attempt artifacts.
Target cleanup runs after failed startup, failed readiness, failed validation, load-tool failure, and successful benchmark cells.
Protocol-specific behavior stays behind validators, load tools, and execution backends rather than inside RunnerEngine.
Console rendering stays in the CLI layer so runner tests and non-CLI hosts can consume structured results directly.
alternatives_considered: Keeping all orchestration in the CLI was rejected because command parsing, target lifecycle, validation, load execution, and reporting are separate runner responsibilities.
Referencing Incursa protocol projects from the runner was rejected because manifests and backend contracts are the implementation-neutral boundary.
Treating raw QUIC as part of this runner extraction was rejected because raw QUIC execution remains incomplete and separately scheduled.
risks: The first extraction may need adapter shims if existing CLI code currently owns too much orchestration state.
Suite resolution and compatibility classification can drift if unsupported cells are silently skipped instead of reported.
Report generation can overstate evidence quality unless validation, load-tool category, local-host warnings, and parser status remain separate fields.

supplemental_sections.heading: CLI Delegation
supplemental_sections.content: The CLI parses arguments, loads repository-root settings, calls RunnerEngine, renders RunnerCommandResult messages and artifacts to the console, and returns the runner exit code. It does not own target lifecycle policy, validation acceptance, load-tool execution semantics, artifact layout decisions, or evidence classification.

supplemental_sections.heading: Host and Presentation Boundary
supplemental_sections.content: RunnerEngine does not reference System.CommandLine or write to the process console. CLI-specific wording and console streams are owned by the CLI renderer, while runner-owned status is represented as RunnerMessage and RunnerEvent records.

supplemental_sections.heading: Protocol-Specific Boundaries
supplemental_sections.content: HTTP, HTTP/3, raw QUIC, WebTransport, MASQUE, and implementation-specific behavior remain behind scenario validators, protocol proof adapters, load-tool manifests, managed load tools, and execution backends. Incursa HTTP/3 and Incursa QUIC can be preferred canonical targets through manifests without becoming runner dependencies.
